title: Cellulitis / skin and soft tissue infection

items.text: Ask about fever, chills, rigors, duration, antipyretic use, and change in symptoms over time.
Ask about localizing respiratory, urinary, gastrointestinal, skin, dental, line, and neurologic symptoms.
Ask about recent antibiotics, prior resistant organisms, recurrent infections, and medication allergies.
Ask about recent hospitalization, procedures, sick contacts, travel, animal or environmental exposures, and food or water exposures.
Ask about diabetes, immunosuppression, malignancy, chronic organ disease, and other infection-risk conditions.
Ask about indwelling devices, wounds, surgery, injections, aspiration risk, and other possible portals of entry.
Ask about onset, rate of spread, pain, itching, drainage, blistering, discoloration, and fever or chills.
Ask about trauma, wounds, bites, injection, surgery, ulcers, tinea, edema, lymphedema, and water exposure.
Ask about rapidly escalating pain, numbness, bullae, systemic toxicity, and pain out of proportion.
Ask about diabetes, vascular disease, immunosuppression, prior MRSA, recurrent cellulitis, and recent antibiotics.
Assess appearance, temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory pattern, oxygenation, and bedside perfusion.
Assess alertness, orientation, capillary refill, extremity temperature, and diaphoresis.
Inspect skin, pressure areas, wounds, intravenous sites, drains, and indwelling devices for focal infection findings.
Inspect work of breathing and auscultate lungs for focal breath-sound changes, crackles, wheeze, or pleural findings.
Inspect and palpate the abdomen and assess for suprapubic or costovertebral-angle tenderness when relevant.
Perform a focused examination of the symptom-directed source, including oral, joint, neurologic, or pelvic findings when indicated.
Inspect and document location, extent, borders, erythema, warmth, tenderness, induration, edema, and drainage.
Assess for fluctuance, crepitus, bullae, necrosis, anesthesia, severe pain, or pain with passive movement.
Inspect for lymphangitic streaking, regional lymphadenopathy, adjacent joint effusion, and range-of-motion limitation.
Assess distal pulses, capillary refill, sensation, motor function, and skin perfusion beyond the affected area.
